item.title: Re: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded:
Posting here for clarity.

The image originates from here, which requires contacting the publishing agency for reusing their materials. As such, the image is noncompliant with CC-BY-SA-3.0 and has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This shows that you can take classic myths and rumors with a odd twist on them and create a scip out of it

item.title: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded: Author, do you happen to have the image rights information for the picture you have in the article? Concern has been raised regarding its use.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This will be long, but is intended just to be thorough.

1- Not a fault of this particular piece, actually, but I find it a little tiring how much MTFs are used. They are, to my eyes, intended as teams whose main asset is mobility. Using them as standard military, as standard special forces, or standard anything, or as more prepared/trained/awesome is something I dislike. I just had to begin with this, but lets move on.

+ show block
– hide block

2- The first paragraph is eight lines long, when it could be three at most. This is overall a problem of the skip. A long read isn't a bad thing by itself, but too long without a purpose becomes tiring quickly, and this achieves to be tiring right at the start and keep being tiring almost all the way.

3- Why is an MTF mobilized to such a menial task as blocking and copying broadcasts? Why so much detail about the work of software designed to achieve what is still a menial task? Why so much detail on the methods used to copy and transport this data? And I thought it was obvious that any field operative should expect punishment for failing at their job. Actually, anyone with a job should.

Those details add nothing to the story being told, just divert from it. Broadcasts are supervised, anomalous instances are blocked and copied for analysis. This is pretty much straightforward, there is nothing unusual or unexpected on the details, and actually they seem mostly unnecessary, since they are kind of irrelevant or, in the case of the USB storage devices, specific for no reason.

4- Second paragraph is even worse. You could say just "evidence should be destroyed per standard procedure, and civilian witnesses administered Class-B amnestics". Everything else just divert from your story, giving unnecessary details and even citing O-5 decision for no real purpose but to say that O-5 can overrule protocol and order atrocities if the shit hits the fan royally. Like they can do with any other SCP.

5- The third paragraph thoroughly states the obvious when it should just read "personnel affected can be treated with Class-C amnestics". The last paragraph on containment makes nothing except state that information about the skip is effectively public internally. It both makes the previous paragraph even more irrelevant (since you will destroy evidence for a population that you provide with evidence) and this skip the most easily known in the entire Foundation.

6- REVISION 3-08 makes it seem that this cognitohazard is somehow more seductive than all the thousand other cognitohazard, to the point where you must tell trained personnel that you shouldn't watch the thing that makes you nuts when you watch it. O-5 themselves had to step out to tell them that this is forbidden. Sorry, but this paragraph is very stupid.

7- First paragraph of Description isn't so bad, actually. It could be trimmed, and the failure rate of the USDPA doesn't seem like a necessary development, but isn't bad either. What it does lack, though, is an actual description. It states that the ad is distorted and full of esoteric messages, but it doesn't really describes nothing about the ad outside there being an "unnamed corporation". Show, don't tell.

8- The bullet-points about the effects put a lot of focus on effects that don't seem that anomalous, actually. It is cumbersome and divert from what really makes the anomaly, well, anomalous. You see, a lot of people believe in a lot of things with "sudden, unexplained interest". It seems more like the descriptions of common things you see in -Js, until far later, when it states that the person effectively evolve this interest into obsession. And the compulsion to spread the anomaly is given so little attention here it isn't really clear until the end of the second Case File.

Put less words in this, and focus those words on what makes your anomaly unique. Make it clear that those UFO-nuts are way more nuts than others. If you want perfectly sane people to be affected, it should be obvious that some of those people shouldn't be into this stuff.

9- Blanking exsanguination just to state it in parenthesis isn't cool. Get out the black boxes if you don't need them. Stating that the number of known subjects is unknown isn't, either, unless the anomaly also blurs the notion of numbers.

10- Case File 01 not only do no good at all to the skip. Creating a useless wall of text just to describe an actual person and use his photo is, AFAIK, against the site policies. You could just add a short one line citation and link the wikipedia page about Robert Lazar:
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bob_Lazar

11- Addendum II, the interview, is a lot better and have some potential, but is still more confusing on its purpose than actually adding a sense of confusion. Who's James Fritz? Why the interviewer is so careless? What is the context of this? It shows that the guy is in negation of what he did, but otherwise I see no reason whatsoever to care about this entire interview, I don't have enough context for this to say anything whatsoever.

12- Addedum III is clearly the centerpiece, and is rightly so. It isn't bad at all. But the previous text already lagged so much I'm tired when I finally reach here. Also, there are some very awkward decisions on the part of the Foundation on this event.

Was there a search for the missing security?

How did the Foundation took 24 minutes to arrive, but just 7 to investigate the scene, cover evidence and lift lock down?

Why did they released O'Connor before gathering more info from him?

What was actually examined and found about Jessica, the bathroom, her office and her body?

Why no one asked why Thomas fled so promptly? He is the only unharmed witness of the entities.

How did Carl West became a medical officer at just 13?


As a courtesy to our readers on mobile devices, please collapse long posts. ~Zyn
.
.
.
And that's it. The last pieces on Carl West can be trimmed a bit, but are overall a good ending. They do need a better context, though, and the text failed on providing this. It still can work, though, just needs a serious rewrite.

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded: I took the liberty of changing the title to [UNTITLED UFO] until the author themselves make a title to the piece. If I stepped out of my right, please make me know, this title is expected to be as neutral as possible while marking the slot as used.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll come back later for a more thorough analysis, but had to downvote it hard. It is too long just because of extremely excessive details. That, and a lot of other issues.

Nothing against the core concept, though.

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Hey, uh, there's no list title for this SCP nor is there one for your other recent SCP, SCP-3822. You can add a list title by going to the Series IV list, scrolling all the way to the bottom, and hitting EDIT. The List pages are just giant articles. Replace the appropriate [ACCESS DENIED] with an article title appropriate to this article.

Thank you!

- Dexanote, Admin

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I changed "amnesiated" to "amnestisized", because I assumed that the author intended to use the common term, which is the latter. As always, feel free to revert if it was an intentional stylistic choice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can someone give this article a name?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty solid overall! One minor nitpick, which I don't blame you for leaving as-is: it's becoming more and more standard to use "amnestitize" when referring to blanking our memories, just as we've mover from using "amnesiacs" to "amnestics". But it's very minor, doesn't hurt anything really. But again, overall, great job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Presumably it'd be something like "[UNTITLED 2]", since SCPs sharing a names gums up the IRC bots, iirc.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Finally got around to reading this and am happy to +1 it, but I should point out that it remains "[ACCESS DENIED]" a month later. If the author can't be reached, we should switch it to "[UNTITLED]" like 3993.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not bad for your first article. Keep it up, and have a an upvote for your efforts.

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded: Heads up, this was successful and it looks like you didn't add a title to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is quite engrossing and I really dig the next-level conspiracy theory angle. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Couple of niggles - the street in San Francisco should San Pablo Avenue, if you meant the actual street. San Francisco Police Department doesn't use Metropolitan in its name (fun fact: SF is both a city and a county with the same borders).

This was quite entertaining. There was enough mystery to make it interesting, but not so much as to render it opaque. I would have liked to have seen a little more of the late stage effects shown rather than told, but it was still a satisfying slab of strange.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
So, this is essentially my take on the "alien conspiracy" that has been prevalent in American culture for a few decades. I could explain everything, but where's the fun in that?

Hope you enjoy!
